column32:
I'm sorry for the negative answers. The SRA does contain very little (if any) domain-specific, that is, requirements engineering specific, components. The SRA could be applied to any text processing task, making it not useful for a requirements engineering tool developer. These generic architectures for NLP already exist (implicitly), see e.g. 
https://dkpro.github.io/
https://www.nltk.org/
column33: IMO, the architecture needs to be less generic and lower the abstraction level to requirements engineering concepts to be of use.

column31: I don't perceive any additional benefits beyond what has already been discussed.
column32:
Beyond what has been discussed, I’d like to highlight a few critical engineering and human factors:
- Low Entry Barrier: A mature SRA should ideally be installable with a single command. It needs a compelling running example that showcases its core strengths and features immediately.
- Documentation & Guardrails: Comprehensive API documentation is a must. More importantly, the SRA should explicitly define its 'non-goals' or anti-patterns—what it does not support or recommend. This prevents developers from wasting effort on ill-suited attempts.
- Inherent Costs: We must acknowledge the learning curve and the higher initial setup cost compared to 'freestyle' development. Furthermore, architectural abstraction often makes debugging more challenging.

column31: I believe that having an SRA for NLP4RE tools would also help the community better understand the complexity of the software we build. Research tools often involve underlying complexity that is not made explicit; however, an SRA can clearly reveal this complexity. In particular, it would allow us to assess how challenging it would be to transition a tool from a research prototype to an industrial setting, and to compare it with successful cases that have already applied the SRA.
column32: I believe the SRA itself would benefit from a clear, concrete example of use involving a specific set of NLP4RE tools. Although the description document explains the main components and the service view of the SRA to some extent, the overall flow between applying the SRA and conceiving the intended tool remains unclear. At this stage, adoption is likely to be challenging if the benefits are not explicitly linked to measurable outcomes. For example, demonstrating that the SRA reduces integration effort by 80-90% (or even eliminates it), or that it increases system understandability by a comparable margin, would make its value more tangible. Such results and evidence-based statements are missing, yet they are essential to properly assess the promise of the SRA for implemented software (basically answering, why should I use the SRA?).
column33: As mentioned previously, the proposal would benefit from a clear end-to-end example demonstrating the use of the SRA. In addition, I believe enriching the package, service, and pipeline views with an explicit goal-oriented perspective would further strengthen the SRA. This would make the intended goals of adopting the SRA; and of the underlying system itself; more explicit, thereby improving clarity, traceability, and overall understanding.
